text: This is a case report of a robotic-assisted Roux-en-Y cystojejunostomy for a large pancreatic pseudocyst. This procedure was performed at Sinai Grace Hospital, part of the Detroit Medical Center. Our patient is a fifty-six-year-old male with chronic recurrent pancreatitis due to excessive alcohol use. He denied any previous surgeries.

Cystic changes were noted on CAT scans as far back as April, evolving and enlarging through July without improvement. His symptoms included epigastric abdominal pain, left upper quadrant pain, nausea, vomiting, and early satiety. During his hospital course, a right upper quadrant ultrasound was completed to rule out gallstones without any cholelithiasis noted.

The CT scan shows a large pancreatic pseudocyst measuring 12.5 by 7 by 11 centimeters, visibly compressed between the left lobe of the liver and the stomach, causing noticeable mass effect and displacement of the stomach.

Preoperatively, our patient was dependent on TPN due to inability to tolerate oral diet. He was sent to subacute rehab for roughly six weeks to allow the cyst to mature and to optimize his nutritional status prior to surgery.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy with the patient in the French position in reverse Trendelenburg. The evaluation reveals the cyst located along the hepatogastric ligament and the lesser curve, rather than posterior to the stomach.
The robotic XI platform is docked, and the port placement is shown with orange indicating eight millimeter trocars and blue indicating twelve millimeter trocars.

The surgeon begins the robotic portion of the procedure by running the small bowel from the ligament of Treitz for forty centimeters. A Maryland bipolar instrument is used to create a small window in the mesenteric border of the bowel.

The surgeon transects the small bowel using a 45mm blue load robotic stapler. The small bowel mesentery is incised with a vessel sealer towards the base to mobilize the Roux limb. The Roux limb is positioned anterior to the transverse colon, adjacent to the cyst, forming an anticholic and antigastric Roux limb. It is sutured to the most dependent portion of the cyst using a 3-0 barbed suture in a running fashion.

A laparoscopic ultrasound is inserted through the assistant port, revealing the cyst with visible septations. The Roux limb is measured to approximately sixty centimeters and positioned adjacent to the biliopancreatic limb, ensuring the mesentery is not twisted. The limbs are sutured together using silk sutures in a seromuscular fashion.

The surgeon creates an aneurotomy on both the Roux limb and the biliopancreatic limb. A 45mm endo-GIA stapler is used to perform an isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis. The common aneurotomy is then closed with a 3-0 running barbed suture in two layers.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to incise the cyst, evacuating chocolate-colored fluid. Due to septations, the cyst is entered for necrosectomy and debridement. The interior of the pseudocyst is visible. Once collapsed, the cavity is irrigated with saline and suctioned. The jejunal aneurotomy is formed at the anti-mesenteric border using monopolar scissors.

The surgeon creates a cystojejunostomy using a running three-oh barbed suture. Intraoperative bowel perfusion assessment is performed on the Roux limb and common channel using near-infrared mode, confirming good blood flow. A fifteen French drain is placed to complete the procedure.

Post-operatively, our patient reported immediate relief of his chronic abdominal pain and his appetite returned almost immediately. He was initiated on a clear liquid diet on post-operative day one and was advanced to a soft diet on post-operative day two, discontinuing his TPN. He was ambulating, urinating, and having bowel function upon discharge on post-operative day two. His JP amylase was negative times two and the JP was removed prior to discharge.

While laparoscopic Roux-en-Y cystojejunostomies are reported in the literature, as shown here with successful outcomes, the use of robotic surgery for this indication is not extensively reported. Our case demonstrates the safe and effective use of robotic surgery for this indication, which allows enhanced articulation for the hand zone anastomosis, better three-D visualization, and increased magnification.
